topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope lay on the kitchen counter, a stark white rectangle mocking me with its finality. My hands were shaking so badly I had to grip the edge of the granite countertop to keep them steady. Liam. Moving across the country. The words echoed in the sudden silence of the apartment, each syllable a tiny hammer blow against my chest. I stared at the address, tracing the unfamiliar street names with a trembling finger. My breath hitched, shallow and quick, as I replayed every shared laugh, every late-night confession, suddenly feeling the warmth of those memories turn icy and brittle. I pushed away from the counter, needing distance, needing to physically put space between myself and this seismic shift in our friendship.

[story 2]

"Are you okay?" Maya asked, her voice laced with a concern that felt like sandpaper on my nerves. I just nodded, a jerky, unconvincing motion, while my eyes darted around the living room, cataloging every object—the worn armchair, the stack of unread books—as if trying to anchor myself to something solid. The news had hit me like a physical blow; the sheer enormity of the distance made my stomach clench with a tight, unpleasant knot. I kept running a hand through my hair, the motion frantic, desperate for some sense of control. "Yeah," I managed, the sound thin and reedy. "Just... processing." The air in the room felt too thick to breathe, heavy with unspoken farewells and the sudden, terrifying void where our daily routine used to be.

[story 3]

I kept staring at my phone screen, the confirmation email glowing with Liam’s new coordinates. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles aching beneath my skin. Every time I tried to focus on something else—the flickering light of the lamp, the distant sound of traffic—my attention snapped back to the screen, cycling through images of packed boxes and unfamiliar landscapes. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the room spin slightly. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of the hallway. My footsteps were sharp and uneven on the wooden floor. I needed air, needed to move, but every direction felt wrong, leading only deeper into this sudden, unwelcome sense of dislocation.
